coredata.dc:title: Inter filter effect between fluorescent copper nanoclusters and Cr(VI) and its application for probing the activity of alkaline phosphatase
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Hongding
Tao, Bei-Bei
Wu, Ning-Ning
Chen, Lin-Ge
Wang, Hai-Bo
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel fluorescent sensing strategy for the detection of alkaline phosphatase activity (ALP) was successfully established using highly luminescent glutathione-stabilized copper nanoclusters (GSH-CuNCs) as fluorescent probe. The GSH-CuNCs were synthesized with GSH as stabilizer and ascorbic acid (AA) as reductant. The optical properties of GSH-CuNCs were further investigated using fluorescence and UV–vis spectrophotometry. And its sensing ability for AA and ALP were explored. The fluorescence intensity of GSH-CuNCs was strongly quenched by Cr(VI) due to the inner filter effect (IFE). However, in the presence of AA, the fluorescence of GSH-CuNCs/Cr(VI) probes was restored due to the weakening of the IFE. It also exhibited a good linear range to the concentration of AA in the range of 0.1–500 μM, and the detection limit was 20 nM. In addition, it has been found that the substrate ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AA2P) had no effect on the fluorescence of the (GSH-CuNCs)-Cr(VI) probe. Since ALP could specifically hydrolyze AA2P into AA, the emission intensity of the probes was restored by an ALP-triggered reaction. As a result, ALP was sensitively and selectively quantified with a wide linear range of 0.1 mU•mL−1 to 200 mU•mL−1 and a low detection limit of 0.02 mU•mL−1. In order to evaluate the practicality of the detection strategy, ALP in real sample analysis was also determined. The results showed that the recoveries and relative standard deviations were acceptable towards the probes. The proposed biosensor could be applied in fields of environmental and biological analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glutathione-stabilized copper nanoclusters
GSH-CuNCs/Cr(VI) probes
Inner filter effect
Alkaline phosphatase activity detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of ALP in human serum samples
Design principle of the fluorescent method for ALP assay
Evaluation of fluorescent assay for ALP detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence signal off–on strategy for the detection of ALP activity by using GSH-CuNCs as probe.

(A) TEM and (B) the corresponding size-distribution histogram of the GSH-CuNCs through analyzing of 100 individual particles; (C) Fluorescence emission (red curve) and excitation (blue curve) spectrum of the as-prepared GSH-CuNCs; (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of the as-prepared GSH-CuNCs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence emission spectra of GSH-CuNCs (a) and GSH-CuNCs in the presence of different substrates such as 200 μM Cr(VI) (b), 500 μM AA + 200 μM Cr(VI) (c), 1000 μM AA2P + 200 μM Cr(VI) (d), and 200 mU/mL ALP + 1000 μM AA2P + 200 μM Cr(VI) (e) in the Tris-HCl buffer solution (20 mM pH = 8.0).

Analytical performance of the assay for AA detection. (A) Fluorescence spectra of CuNCs-Cr (VI) probes in the presence of a serial of AA concentrations (from left to right: 0, 0.1, 1, 10, 20, 50, 75, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450, 500, 600, 800 μM); (B) The selectivity of sensing system for AA detection. Herein, the F refer to the fluorescence intensity of CuNCs-Cr (VI) probes in the presence of AA and other interferes, respectively. The error bars stand for the standard deviation of three repetitive measurements.

Analytical performance of the assay for ALP detection. (A) Fluorescence spectra of CuNCs-Cr (VI) probes under various ALP activities (from right to left: 0, 0.1, 1, 10, 20, 50, 100, 120, 150, 200, 250, 300 mU·mL−1) and AA2P; (B) The selectivity of the proposed assay for ALP activity detection. The concentration of each enzyme including trypsin, GOx, thrombin, lysozyme, HRP, AChE, and ALP were 200 mU·mL−1, the concentrations of BSA was 1 mg·mL−1. The error bars stand for the standard deviation of three repetitive measurements.
Determination of ALP in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Inter filter effect between fluorescent copper nanoclusters and Cr(VI) and its application for probing the activity of alkaline phosphatase

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xinyang Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterials for Bioanalysis, Institute for Conservation and Utilization of Agro-bioresources in Dabie Mountains, Xinyang Normal University, Xinyang 464000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Xinyang Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterials for Bioanalysis
Institute for Conservation and Utilization of Agro-bioresources in Dabie Mountains

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xinyang Key Laboratory of Functional Nanomaterials for Bioanalysis, Institute for Conservation and Utilization of Agro-bioresources in Dabie Mountains, Xinyang Normal University, Xinyang 464000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence signal off–on strategy for the detection of ALP activity by using GSH-CuNCs as probe.

Herein, a novel fluorescent sensing strategy for the detection of alkaline phosphatase activity (ALP) was successfully established using highly luminescent glutathione-stabilized copper nanoclusters (GSH-CuNCs) as fluorescent probe. The GSH-CuNCs were synthesized with GSH as stabilizer and ascorbic acid (AA) as reductant. The optical properties of GSH-CuNCs were further investigated using fluorescence and UV–vis spectrophotometry. And its sensing ability for AA and ALP were explored. The fluorescence intensity of GSH-CuNCs was strongly quenched by Cr(VI) due to the inner filter effect (IFE). However, in the presence of AA, the fluorescence of GSH-CuNCs/Cr(VI) probes was restored due to the weakening of the IFE. It also exhibited a good linear range to the concentration of AA in the range of 0.1–500 μM, and the detection limit was 20 nM. In addition, it has been found that the substrate ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AA2P) had no effect on the fluorescence of the (GSH-CuNCs)-Cr(VI) probe. Since ALP could specifically hydrolyze AA2P into AA, the emission intensity of the probes was restored by an ALP-triggered reaction. As a result, ALP was sensitively and selectively quantified with a wide linear range of 0.1 mU•mL−1 to 200 mU•mL−1 and a low detection limit of 0.02 mU•mL−1. In order to evaluate the practicality of the detection strategy, ALP in real sample analysis was also determined. The results showed that the recoveries and relative standard deviations were acceptable towards the probes. The proposed biosensor could be applied in fields of environmental and biological analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most essential hydrolases that is most effective in an alkaline environment in all body tissues, alkaline phosphatase (ALP) plays an important role in normal growth [1]. The concentration of serum ALP in adults is 46–190 U/L, while the content of ALP in pregnant women and children is found to be greater than 500 U/L [2]. However, abnormal expression of ALP levels in human serum is highly relevant to various diseases including breast carcinoma, liver dysfunction bone disease and diabetes [3–5]. Additionally, because of its high turnover number, high stability, and broad substrate specificity, ALP is also widely utilized as a labeling enzyme to produce detectable signal in immunoassay, DNA hybridization analysis and protein detection [6–8]. Therefore, sensitive detection of ALP activity is of significance in various diagnostic applications.

Considering the importance of ALP detection in disease diagnosis, many analytical methods such as colorimetry [9,10], fluorescence [11], chemiluminescence [12], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [13], electrochemistry [14,15], and chromatography [16] have been developed for monitoring the ALP activity. Although each of these methods possesses their own features and gives the different insights for ALP sensing, many of them generally suffer from some disadvantages including poor photostability and solubility in water, time-consuming, high cost, sophisticated synthesis process or surface modification. Among these methods, the fluorescent method has the merits of high reliability and sensitivity, simple operation, fast response speed, real-time analysis, and convenient instrument requirements, and it is widely used for the analysis and detection of complex samples [17–20].

In recent years, several fluorescent sensing platforms have been designed for ALP detection [21–24]. However, most of them are based on the signal turn-off modes. Such modes are always associated with low signal outputs, formidable background, and relatively low sensitivity and selectivity [25]. Moreover, these signal turn-off strategies may increase the probability of generating high false-positive signals, leading to poor accuracy [26,27]. By contrast, the fluorescence switch-on assays have attracted overwhelming attention due to the superior properties such as fewer false signals, excellent selectivity and enhanced sensitivity. Thus, the exploration of signal turn-on fluorescent method for sensitive detection of ALP is still highly desirable.

Actually, various fluorescent indicators including organic dyes, coordination polymers, and functional nanomaterials such as carbon dots, gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), polydopamine nanoparticles, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), and so on, have been utilized to assess the ALP activity with turn-on fluorescence response [28–36]. However, the synthesis, modification and purification of organic dyes and coordination polymers are typically tedious and time consuming. Although carbon dots can be synthesized by green routes, they always involve in using microwaves. AuNCs have good optical stability for fluorescent sensing, but they suffer from the disadvantages of relative long time (more than 10 h) and high cost. Although QDs have strong photoluminescence emission, the shortage of toxicity impede their further application. Undoubtedly, it is imperative to develop a facile, green, low cost and low toxicity nanomaterial for detecting ALP activity based on the fluorescent turn-on modes.

Herein, we report a facile one-step green approach to synthesis fluorescent GSH-CuNCs by employing GSH as stabilizer and AA as reductant. The as-prepared GSH-CuNCs have good optical stability and exhibit strong fluorescence emission at 420 nm under excitation wavelength of 365 nm. A fluorescent signal “turn–on” assay is established for probing the activity of ALP with high sensitivity and selectivity using the prepared GSH-CuNCs as fluorescent probe. The fluorescence of GSH-CuNCs was initially quenched by Cr(VI) and could be restored by introducing AA to the GSH-CuNCs/Cr(VI) system. It was ascribed to that Cr(VI) was reduced to Cr(III) by AA, which leading to the IFE existed in the GSH-CuNCs/Cr(VI) probes was inhibited. As we all know, ALP can catalyze the substrate AA2P hydrolysis and dephosphorylation into AA. Thus, the ALP activity could be detected indirectly according to the fluorescence recovery of GSH-CuNCs. The strategy was a signal turn-on assay and could effectively reduce the false positives. In addition, this strategy may extend the application of metal nanoclusters (NCs) in biological reactions.

Copper (II) sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O), ascorbic acid (AA), ascorbic acid 2-phosphate (AA2P), potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) and other chemical (analytical grade) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich Chemical Co. (St. Louis, USA). Bovine serum albumin (BSA), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), acetylcholinesterase (AChE), glucose oxidase (GOx), lysozyme (Lys), thrombin, trypsin, uric acid (UA), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), glutathione (GSH), cysteine (Cys), dopamine (DA), and glycine (Gly) were obtained from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). In this work, ultrapure water (>18.2 MΩ•cm) produced from a purification system (Millipore Milli-Q, Billerica, USA) was utilized to prepared the solutions.

The fluorescence emission spectra from 390 nm to 600 nm were collected on a fluorescence spectrometer of Hitachi F-7000 (Japan) at room temperature with excitation wavelength at 365 nm. Both the excitation and emission slits were set as 5.0 nm. The UV–vis absorption spectra were performed on a spectrophotometer of Hitachi U-3900 (Japan). A emission transmission electron microscopy with a model of Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN field (America) was employed to collect transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the as-prepared GSH-CuNCs. A X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) with a mode of Thermo Scientific K-ALPHA X-ray (America) was used to perform the X-ray photoelectron spectra of GSH-CuNCs. An infrared spectroscopy with a mode of Bruker Tensor 27 was employed to record Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) of GSH-CuNCs.

GSH-CuNCs were synthesized in the light of the previously reported methods [37]. Firstly, 40 mL of 0.21 mM GSH solution was added into 125 μL of 50 mM CuSO4 solution under vigorous stirring and the mixture was incubated for 5 min at room temperature. The mixed solution gradually became turbid, owing to the formation of copper thiolate complexes. After that, 900 μL of 100 mM freshely-prepared AA solution was injected into the mixture. After the mixture was reacted at 65 ◦C with stirring for 4 h, the turbid solution slowly changed to clear. Then, the solution was cooled to room temperature in air and purified by centrifugation at 16000 rpm for 10 min. The obtained supernatant was dialyzed against ultra-pure water for 48 h in a dialysis bag with a cutoff of 100–500 Da. Finally, the dialyzed GSH-CuNCs were collected and stored at 4 ◦C for the next use.

The constructed procedure of GSH-CuNCs-Cr(VI) probes was described as following: 20 μL of 500 μM Cr(VI) was firstly mixed with 20 μL of the obtained GSH-CuNCs, then 20 μL of Tris-HCL solutions (50 mM, pH 8.0) and 40 μL of ultrapure water were injected into the above mixture. After the obtained mixture was blended with a quick blender for 10 min, the GSH-CuNCs-Cr(VI) probes were finally obtained.

Typically, 10 μL of AA solutions with various concentrations (0, 0.1, 1, 10, 20, 50, 75, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400 μM) and 20 μL of 1 mM Cr(VI) were mixed and blended quickly. After the mixture was incubated at 25 ℃ in water bath for 8 min, 20 μL of GSH-CuNCs, 20 μL of Tris-HCl buffer solutions (50 mM, pH 8.0) and 30 μL of ultrapure water were added into the above mixture. Finally, the fluorescence emission spectra of the sensing system from 390 nm to 600 nm were collected with excitation wavelength of 365 nm. Both the excitation and emission slits were set as 5.0 nm.

For the detection of ALP activity, 10 μL of different concentrations of ALP (0.5, 2, 10, 25, 40, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150 mU•mL−1) and 10 μL of 10 mM AA2P were added into 10 μL of Tris-HCl solutions (50 mM, pH 8.0) and 20 μL of ultrapure water. After the mixture was incubated at 37 ℃ for 40 min, 20 μL of 1 mM Cr(VI) solution was put into the solution and then incubated at 25 ℃ for 8 min. After that, 20 μL of GSH-CuNCs and 10 μL of Tris-HCl solutions (50 mM, pH 8.0) were added into above reaction solution. Finally, the fluorescent emission spectrum of the sensing system was recorded under the excitation wavelength at 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ALP in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum samples were obtained from healthy volunteers provided by the Hospital of Xinyang Normal University. The serum samples were first centrifuged by 1500 rpm for 10 min and the impurities were removed. Then, the supernatant was diluted to 50 times with Tris-HCl solutions before use. In real sample assay, a serum sample was added instead of the standard ALP solution and the experiment was performed same as the above ALP activity assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design principle of the fluorescent method for ALP assay
ce:section.ce:para: The design principle of fluorescent bioassay was shown in Fig. 1 . At the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, GSH-CuNCs has a strong fluorescence emission at 420 nm. After the Cr(VI) is introduced, the fluorescent signal of GSH-CuNCs is significantly quenched, owing to the large spectrum overlapping between the absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) and the excitation spectra of GSH-CuNCs. This mode may be assigned to the IFE (Inner Filter Effect) between Cr(VI) and GSH-CuNCs. However, the fluorescence intensity of GSH-CuNCs is recovered in the presence of AA. It is assigned to that AA reduces Cr(VI) to Cr(III), which destroys the IFE between Cr(VI) and GSH-CuNCs. In this case, the content of AA can be detected by the fluorescence change of the sensing system. In addition, ALP can catalyze the substrate AA2P to form AA that reduces Cr(VI) to Cr(III), leading to the fluorescence intensity change of GSH-CuNCs. Therefore, the activity of ALP can be indirectly detected according to the fluorescence change of GSH-CuNCs caused by the amount of enzymatically produced AA.

First, the synthesized GSH-CuNCs were evaluated by TEM images. As shown in Fig. 1A, most of the GSH-CuNCs are spherical and evenly distributed in size. TEM image analysis (Fig. 2 B) shows that the size of about 100 particles are estimated to be 4.14 ± 0.64 nm (Fig. 2B). The GSH-CuNCs were further characterized by XPS and FTIR. As shown in Fig. S1, two prominent peaks can be observed at 952.5 and 932.8 eV in the XPS spectra, which correspond to Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2 of Cu0, respectively. Meanwhile, no satellite peak is observed at 942 eV, indicating that no Cu2+ are presented in the system. These results confirm that Cu2+ are completely reduced to GSH-CuNCs. The FTIR spectra of GSH-Cu NCs is shown in Fig. S2. Compared with FTIR spectra of free GSH, the absorption band around 2520 cm−1 that corresponds to the -SH stretching vibration disappears in the FTIR spectra of GSH-Cu NCs, demonstrating the formation of Cu-S bonds. These results confirm the successful preparation of GSH-Cu NCs. The optical properties of GSH-Cu NCs were also studied by fluorescence spectra and UV–Vis absorption spectra. As for Fig. 2C, the as-prepared GSH-CuNCs show the maximum fluorescence emission intensity noticed at 420 nm (red line) with excitation wavelength of 365 nm (blue line). The UV–Vis absorption spectra of GSH-CuNCs dispersed in water is shown in Fig. 2D. The results show that there is a strong absorption at 294 nm (UV region) and no obvious absorption is observed above 400 nm (visible light region). Moreover, the fluorescence quantum yield of GSH-CuNCs is determined to be 8.4%.

In addition, to investigate the stability of GSH-CuNCs, the luminescence intensity of GSH-CuNCs under different experimental conditions was studied. First of all, the effect of pH value of buffer solutions (from 6.0 to 10.0) on the emission intensity of GSH-CuNCs were assessed. As displayed in Fig. S3A, the fluorescence intensity doesn’t change significantly when pH is in the range of 6.0–10.0. In addition, temperature was also an important factor to survey the luminescent stability of nanomaterials. It demonstrates that no significant changes in the emission intensity of the GSH-CuNCs are observed with increasing temperature (Fig. S3B), implying that the luminescence intensity of GSH-CuNCs remains stable even the ambient temperature has changed. In addition, it displays that the fluorescence intensity of GSH-CuNCs remains about 93% after 3 months storage at 4℃. These results confirm that stable GSH-CuNCs can be used as a fluorescence probe for the establishment of sensing methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of fluorescent assay for ALP detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further assess the feasibility of fluorescent assay of determining AA and ALP, the fluorescence response of GSH-CuNCs under different experimental conditions was studied. As it can be seen from Fig. 3 , GSH-CuNCs possess a strong emission peak at 420 nm with the excitation wavelength of 365 nm (curve a). When 200 μΜ Cr(VI) is introduced to the sensing system (curve b), over 86.72% fluorescence signal of GSH-CuNCs is dramatically quenched. Amusingly, the fluorescence recovery rate of GSH-CuNCs reaches 64% in the presence of 500 μΜ AA (curve c). Notably, it also displays that substrate AA2P has no obvious effect on the fluorescence of the reaction system (curve d). However, the emission intensity can be restored by 63% when the substrate AA2P and ALP are introduced simultaneously (curve e). These results show that this fluorescent assay is feasible to detect AA and ALP activity.

In order to obtain the better fluorescence quenching efficiency of GSH-CuNCs by Cr(VI), the experimental conditions for the reaction of GSH-CuNCs with Cr(VI) were optimized firstly. As shown in Fig. S4A, in the pH range from 6.0 to 10.0, the Cr(VI) displays a higher quenching effect in alkaline conditions than that in the acidic conditions. Moreover, the quenching effect shows no significant difference in alkaline conditions. Considering the subsequent application of the fluorescent assay, pH value of 7.5 is chosen as the optimized pH value. In addition, to evaluate the effect of reaction time on quenching effect, the quenching effect of Cr(VI) to GSH-CuNCs are evaluated after being incubated for different time by measuring the fluorescence intensity of GSH-CuNCs. As swhon in Fig. S4B, it verifies that reaction time has no appreciable effect on the quenching effect. Finally, the influence of temperature on the quenching effect was investigated. Fig. S4C indicates that the fluorescence quenching of GSH-CuNCs by Cr(VI) is independent of temperature.

Under optimum experimental conditions, the effect of the Cr(VI) concentrations on the GSH-CuNCs was studied. As shown in Fig. S5A, it demonstrates that the fluorescence signal of GSH-CuNCs decreases gradually when the Cr(VI) concentrations increased from 0 to 350 μM. Nearly 87% of fluorescence of GSH-CuNCs is quenched in the existence of 200 μM Cr(VI). As it can be seen from Fig. S5B, the quenching efficiency of Cr(VI) is positively correlated with its concentration, where quenching efficiency can be defined as (F0 - F)/F0. Herein, F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of GSH-CuNCs before and after addition of Cr(VI), repsctively. While the Cr(VI) concentration exceeds 200 μM, the quenching efficiency changes a little. Therefore, 200 μM of Cr(VI) is used in the follow-up experiments to prepare GSH-CuNCs-Cr(VI) probes.

To obtain excellent sensitivity, different reaction conditions (such as the reaction time and enzyme catalysis time) that effect on the sensing performance for AA and ALP are optimized. As depicted in Fig. S6A, the fluorescence intensity increases with the reaction time (from 0 min to 8 min) and remains unchanged after 8 min. Therefore, 8 min is chosen as the optimal reaction time to determine the content of AA. The influence of enzyme catalysis time of reactive system for ALP sensing is clarified in Fig. S6B. The fluorescence intensity enhances in the range of 0–40 min, while it changes no obviously from 40 to 60 min. Thus, 40 min is selected as the optimum reaction time for the determination of ALP.

Under the optimized experimental conditions, the AA was detected quantitatively. Fig. 4 A shows the typical emission spectra of fluorescence probes in presence of different concentrations of AA. From the bottom to the top, it can be observed the fluorescence intensity increases of GSH-CuNCs with the concentration of AA ranging from 0 to 500 μM. The fluorescence recovery is mainly attributed to the AA mediated reduction between Cr(VI) and Cr(III), which results in the destruction of IFE between GSH-CuNCs and Cr(VI). Fig. S7 indicates a decent linear relationship between F/F0 and AA concentration (from 0.1 μM to 500 μM), the linear equation is fitted as F/F0 = 0.0074CAA + 0.9504 (R2 = 0.9943). Here, F and F0 are defined as the emission intensity of the probes with and without AA at 420 nm, respectively. The limit of detection for AA is calculated to be 20 nM (S/N = 3), which is compatible or even better than the reported references [38–40].

To evaluate the selectivity of the CuNCs-Cr(VI) system based on IFE for AA assay, the fluorescence responses toward various interfering substances such as ions, biomolecules and reductants were studied. As shown in Fig. 4C, it indicates that the F/F0 of the system in the presence of AA increases dramatically. However, in the presence of other interfering substances at the same concentration, there is no significant fluorescence response. The above results confirm that this method possesses a good selectivity for AA detection.

Taking advantage of excellent analytical performance toward AA, the sensing system was utilized for monitoring ALP activity. The ALP possesses the capability of catalyzing the hydrolysis of AA2P to AA, the generated AA can effectively reduce Cr (VI) to Cr (III). Thereby the IFE between GSH-CuNCs and Cr (VI) is effectively destroyed, which can restore the fluorescence of the GSH-CuNCs probes. Thus, the ALP activity can be detected indirectly according to the fluorescence recovery of GSH-CuNCs. As anticipated, the fluorescence signal of the probes shows an increasing behavior depended on the ALP activity. Along with the concentrations of ALP increasing from 0 to 300 mU•mL−1, the fluorescence intensity at 420 nm rises gradually (depicted in Fig. 5 A). In the activity concentration range from 0.1 to 200 mU•mL−1, the F/F0 is proportional to the concentration of ALP activity and the plot is fitted as F/F0 = 0.0188CALP + 1.0196 (R2 = 0.9984) (shown in Fig. S8). And it is worthwhile to mention that the detection limits is calculated to be 0.02 mU•mL−1 (S/N = 3). As demonstrated in Table S1, it indicates that the proposed bioassay performs well with an acceptable detection limit and linear range compared with previous approaches [40–44].

To prove the selectivity of ALP assay, the fluorescence intensity of the probes existed with other non-specific proteins/enzymes, including trypsin, GOx, thrombin, lysozyme, HRP, AChE, BSA and ALP were measured. As shown in Fig. 5C, the emission intensity of the probes is the highest in the presence of ALP. While the emission intensity of GSH-CuNCs in the presence of other disturbances shows no significant difference compared to the blank experiment (without ALP). The results indicate that the proposed ALP assay exhibits a high selectivity toward ALP detection, which is beneficial to the determination of ALP in biological samples.

The practical application of the bioassay in real samples was tested by detecting ALP activity in 50-fold diluted human serum samples. As shown in Table 1 , the content of ALP in the 50-fold diluted human serum is 1.5 mU⋅mL−1, corresponding to 75 mU⋅mL−1 of ALP content in initial human serum. The result is consistent with the normal levels of ALP in healthy human reported by the previous literature [45]. Moverever, the application capability of the proposed method was further evaluated by measuring ALP activity in diluted human serum through standard addition method. The recoveries are changed from 98.94% to 100.6%, and relative standard deviations (RSD) are in the range from 3.7% to 4.6% (n = 3), proving the application capability of this method in complicated biological samples analysis.

To ascertain the sensing mechanism, the excitation and emission spectra of GSH-CuNCs and absorption spectra of Cr(VI) were measured firstly. If spectral overlap occurs, the quenching mechanism will be considered as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or IFE firstly. As displayed in Fig. S9A, there is no overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectra of Cr(VI) and emission spectra of GSH-CuNCs, which indicates that the possibility of FRET process between GSH-CuNCs and Cr(VI) can be excluded. Since the occurrence of FRET requires the large overlap between the emission spectrum of the fluorophore (donor) and the absorption spectrum of the quencher (acceptor) [46]. As for IFE process, a good spectral overlap should be existed between the absorption spectrum of a quencher and the excitation/emission spectrum of fluorescent probe [47]. As exhibited in Fig. S9A, the absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) and the excitation spectra of GSH-CuNCs overlapped largely, speculating the IFE occurred between Cr(VI) and GSH-CuNCs. In order to further understand the sensing mechanism for AA and ALP, the absorption spectra of Cr(VI) in the absence and presence of AA were measured respectively. As depicted in Fig. S9B, the fluorescence excitation spectra of GSH-CuNCs overlaps well with absorption spectra of Cr(VI). This results in the IFE between Cr(VI) and GSH-CuNCs, which causes the fluorescence quenching of GSH-CuNCs. However, the UV absorption peak belonged to Cr (VI) disappears obviously after introducing AA, it is attributed to that Cr(VI) is reduced to Cr(III) by AA. In consequence, the IFE is greatly limited and the fluorescence of GSH-CuNCs recovers. The above results prove that the sensing mechanism for the detection of AA and ALP is to efficiently weaken the IFE between Cr(VI) and GSH-CuNCs.

In summary, a fluorescent assay was reported for the determination of ALP activity using CuNC-Cr(VI) as probes. The Cr(VI) can be reduced by AA to form Cr(III), weakening the IFE in the CuNC-Cr(VI) probes and further restoring the fluorescence intensity of GSH-CuNCs. The proposed method displayed good performance for the detection of AA and ALP activity. In addition, it was successfully applied to detect ALP activity in human serum samples. The method showed good advantages of simple sample treatment, low solvent consumption, low cost and high sensitivity. It has a broad application prospect in drug screening, early biology and clinical diagnosis.

Hongding Zhang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Bei-Bei Tao: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Ning-Ning Wu: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Lin-Ge Chen: Investigation, Validation, Data curation. Hai-Bo Wang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U1704153), 1000 Talents Program of Zhongyuan (ZYQR201912153), High-level Talent in Henan Province (202101JY0940C), Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (No. 222300420273), Nanhu Scholars Program for Young Scholars of XYNU.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109066.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A sensitive colorimetric and fluorescent sensor based on imidazolium-functionalized squaraines for the detection of GTP and alkaline phosphatase in aqueous solution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N.J. Wu, J.B. Lan, L.P. Yan, J.S. You, A sensitive colorimetric and fluorescent sensor based on imidazolium-functionalized squaraines for the detection of GTP and alkaline phosphatase in aqueous solution, Chem. Commun. 50 (2014) 4438-4441.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.U. Hausamen, R. Helger, W. Rick, W. Gross, Optimal conditions for the determination of serum alkaline phosphatase by a new kinetic method, Clin. Chim. Acta 15 (1967) 241-245.
R.G. Michael, J. Sambrook, Alkaline Phosphatase, Cold Spring Harb Protoc. 8 (2020) 100768.

Nanomed. 7 (2012) 4159.
